summary: DeVault argues that link rot has become an axiom of the internet, accelerated not just by expired domains but by DMCA abuse, paywalls, region locking, and platform shutdowns. He cites statistics showing roughly 5% of links die per year and half of URLs in Supreme Court opinions are already dead. His response is to personally archive anything he wants to see twice — YouTube channels, RSS feeds, websites, git repos, and all media in DRM-free form. He closes with a call to action: don't build platforms that trap user data, give people DRM-free downloads, and let archive.org do its job.
key_points: Link rot is accelerating due to DMCA abuse, region locking, paywalls, and platform collapses — not just server failures
Users who trust platforms with their content lose it by the millions when those platforms die or change policies
DeVault's personal solution is aggressive offline archiving of everything via cron jobs, wget, and local mirrors
Content creators and platform operators have a responsibility to enable archival rather than obstruct it
money_quotes: Today, link rot is on the rise under the influence of more sinister factors.
Users rely on platforms to host content and links rot by the millions when the platforms die.
At this point, link rot is an axiom of the internet.
When I see a cool YouTube video I like, I archive the entire channel right away.
I have DRM free offline copies of all of my music, TV shows, and movies, ill-begotten or not.
Don't build unsustainable platforms and ask users to trust you with their data.
If you run a website, let archive.org scrape it.
And archive anything you want to see again.
key_insight: Link rot is no longer an accident but a systemic feature of platform-controlled internet, and the only reliable defense is aggressive personal archiving.
